resources.string: We saved your workout. You can finish and save it anytime from the dashboard.
Pick your experience level — we’ll recommend exercises that fit you best.
Heavy work + volume in the same session.
More of your work leans toward strength stimulus.
More of your work leans toward muscle-building stimulus.
More of your work leans toward endurance / density.
If it felt too easy: add 1 hard set to your main lift or push closer to failure.
Simple structure: 1–3 heavy sets + 2–4 back-off sets (6–10 reps).
Want a clearer profile next time? Choose one knob: heavier / more sets / shorter rests.
Keep heavy sets crisp: longer rests, fewer junk sets, track top sets.
To push strength: add a heavier top set, then reduce accessories.
To make it clearly strength-focused: lower reps, longer rests, fewer exercises.
Stay consistent: 6–12 reps, stable volume, progress via reps or load.
To push size: add 1–2 hard sets to key muscles and keep reps controlled.
To make it clearly hypertrophy-focused: more working sets in 6–12 reps, shorter rests.
Density is king: shorter rests, keep pace, avoid long downtime.
To push endurance: slightly higher reps and reduce rest times.
To make it clearly endurance-focused: higher reps, shorter rests, keep pace.
Ranked by their share of total training load. Load = sets × reps × weight, adjusted by intensity (%% of 1RM).
Showing top %1$d of %2$d exercises. The rest contributed less to the load.
All %1$d exercises shown, ordered by contribution.
Share of total training load landed on each muscle, weighted by how each exercise targets it.
How your load splits by exercise type and by pushing/pulling pattern.
Compound: multi-joint (squat, bench, row). Isolation: single-joint (curl, extension).
Push: press/extend (bench, OHP). Pull: pull toward you (row, pull-up). Hinge: hip-dominant (deadlift, RDL).
Not enough data yet — log more sets to see the breakdown.
Where the training stimulus landed across the three pillars of training.
Heavy reps near a top set
Mid-range reps in the muscle-building zone
A read-out of how today\'s work shaped up — what you did, where it landed, and what to notice.
There wasn\'t enough working stimulus to classify a style today. That\'s fine for technique or recovery days.
Strength, hypertrophy and endurance all showed up. A more specific session is usually a stronger stimulus.
Heavy work + volume in the same session — strength and size both got worked.
One pillar dominates this period — the stimulus was specific and easy to attribute.
A direction is showing but the gap to the runner-up is small — easy to drift the other way next session.
Most of the load came from multi-joint lifts — efficient, transferable, and easy to progress.
Single-joint lifts carried most of the load. Compounds let you do more with less time.
The top exercise alone accounted for a large slice of the load. Variety usually means better recovery and more balanced gains.
No single exercise dominated — load was spread across multiple lifts.
One direction did most of the work. Balancing push and pull over time protects the shoulders.
Most of the load went to just two muscles. Useful as specialization, costly if it\'s every session.
Anchor the next session with a compound (squat, bench, row, deadlift).
Swap in another exercise instead of stacking sets on one.
Add a pulling movement next session — row or pull-up.
Add a pressing movement next session — bench or overhead.
Cover more groups next session — legs, back, or core.
Add 1 hard set or push closer to failure to make it count.
Pick one knob next session: heavier, more sets, or shorter rests.
Repeat this load once before moving up.
Usual gap ~%1$d days; alert at %2$d days
Add it back this week if recovery allows.
Active in %1$d of %2$d weeks, %3$d total sessions
Same setup next time and go for it.
🔥 You’re doing great. Keep it up.
It’s okay to pause. Come back anytime.
🚀 Start fresh. One step is enough.
Shows how your workout duration changes over time. A rising line means sessions are getting longer; a falling line means you’re finishing faster.
Shows how much work you do per workout. A rising line means more work; several drops in a row may mean it’s time to ease up or add rest.
Your work rate — how much volume you complete per minute. Higher means more work in the same time; lower means a slower or more rest-heavy session.
Total reps per workout. A rising line means you’re handling more work; several drops in a row often signal fatigue.
Average working weight per weighted rep. Higher means heavier working loads on average.
Add your first exercise to start tracking this workout.
No sets yet. Add one to start tracking.
Your account and data will be permanently removed. You won’t be able to restore them later.
Personalized workouts for your level, gym, and progress.
Create an account to save workouts and track progress.
This helps Grippo personalize your workouts.
How long have you been training?
Turn off muscle groups you don’t want to train.
Turn off unavailable equipment and we’ll hide exercises that need it.
Used to personalize recommendations and body-weight calculations.
Used to track BMI and calibrate body-weight exercises.
Less than 1 year of training. Still getting familiar with the main exercises.
Over 1 year of training. Comfortable with most exercises and have a solid base.
Over 2 years of training. Good form and steady physical progress.
Over 5 years of training. Excellent form and a high level of muscular development.
Workout tips, product updates, and practical training ideas.
Shows how evenly your muscles were trained.
Start your first workout to see highlights here.
Closing now will discard all progress from this session.
Nice work. Ready to wrap it up?
You have an unfinished workout saved on this device. Continue where you left off?
You left some changes unsaved while editing this workout. Continue editing?
Reps: how many reps you did in this set. Weight: follow the tips below.
Weight: if there is no added weight, enter 1. If you hold a plate/dumbbell/vest, enter added weight only.
Enter the total for the pair (2×20 → 40), even for alternating reps.
Enter total weight on the bar. If you include the bar, include it every time.
Hold the V-handle with both hands.
Hold the wide handle with both hands.
Hold the close handle with both hands.
Enter total weight on the EZ-bar. If you include the bar, include it every time.
Enter total weight on the trap bar. If you include the bar, include it every time.
Hold the rope with one or two hands — as the exercise requires.
Hold the straight bar with both hands.
Hold the handle with one hand or as a pair — as the exercise requires.
Enter the weight you set on the machine.
Enter the machine weight. If left/right show different numbers, use one side.
Enter the machine weight. If left/right show different numbers, use one side.
Enter the weight you set on leg extensions.
Enter the weight you set on leg curls.
Enter the weight you set on the chest press machine.
Enter the weight you set on the biceps machine.
Enter total plates. The Smith bar itself can be skipped — just be consistent every time.
Enter total plates on the hack squat.
Enter total plates on the leg press.
Enter total plates on the deadlift machine.
Enter the weight you set on the shoulder machine.
Enter the weight you set on the lateral raise machine.
Enter the weight you set on the triceps machine.
Enter the weight you set on the calf machine.
Enter the weight you set on the glute machine.
Enter the weight you set on lat pulldown.
Enter the weight you set on the cable station.
On crossover, enter the weight for one side.
Enter the weight you set on seated row.
No extra weight — enter your bodyweight. With a belt/vest — add the extra weight.
No extra weight — enter your bodyweight. With a belt/vest — add the extra weight.
No extra weight — enter your bodyweight. Holding a plate/dumbbell — add it.
No extra weight — enter your bodyweight. With a plate/vest — add it.
If you use external weight, enter its weight.
If you use external weight, enter its weight.
Enter the weight you set on the adductor machine.
Enter the weight you set on the abductor machine.
If you use external weight, enter its weight.
Enter total barbell weight on the rack.
Enter total barbell weight on the rack.
Enter total barbell weight on the rack.
Enter total barbell weight in the rack.
Enter the weight you lift in the exercise.
Enter the weight you use in the exercise.
Come back to finish or save your progress.
A fresh weight keeps your progress and recommendations accurate.
When do you want to reach it?
What are you working toward right now? We’ll shape the experience around it.
Train for muscle growth with structured volume and steady progression.
Focus on heavier lifts, clear progression, and strength-focused work.
Support fat loss with consistent, muscle-preserving training.
Rebuild your routine gradually with controlled progression.
Maintain your fitness with balanced, repeatable workouts.
Build a balanced routine for strength, movement, and consistency.
Add muscle on the side of your main goal.
Build strength alongside your main focus.
Support fat loss alongside your main goal.
Rebuild your base gradually while pursuing your main goal.
Hold your current level without losing what you’ve built.
Improve energy, endurance, and overall fitness.
Build a lasting training habit, week after week.
Log a few more workouts so we can score adherence.
Shows how closely your recent work matches your goal.
A closer look at how your training matches the goal you picked.
Log a few workouts in this period so we can score adherence.
Your goal just started — a stable pattern usually takes about a week of training to show up.
You\'re past the original target. Consider extending the deadline or wrapping the goal up.
You\'re in the final stretch. Lock in the pattern that has been working.
Your training matched the plan through the full period — nice work.
Your recent training pattern matches the goal you chose.
Some sessions don\'t fit the pattern this goal needs. Small adjustments will get you back.
Most of your recent work goes toward a different quality than what this goal needs.
Low-rep, high-load sets are the key driver of getting stronger, and they\'re a small share of your volume.
A clear majority of your work is heavy enough to build strength.
Moderate-load, moderate-rep sets (6–12 reps) drive muscle growth — you\'re not doing enough of them.
Most of your work lives in the hypertrophy rep range.
Fat-loss goals benefit from higher reps and shorter rest — you\'re leaning heavy instead.
You\'re keeping the pace high enough to drive the conditioning you need.
This goal rewards a rough 50/50 split between the two core qualities — you\'re leaning into one.
The two core qualities are trading weight evenly, which is what this goal needs.
A third quality is crowding in
The quality this goal wants suppressed is taking up too much of your session time.
You\'re training below the minimum work needed to rebuild capacity. Add a couple more light sessions.
Volume and intensity are already high for a return phase. Pull back to protect recovery.
You\'re training enough to rebuild without overshooting.
Work in 3–5 reps at 85%+ of your 1RM on a main compound lift.
Target 8–12 reps at 65–75% 1RM and do 3–4 sets per muscle group.
Try shorter rests, higher reps (12–20), and circuit-style finishers.
Plan one heavy lower-rep day and one moderate-rep day each week.
Alternate a strength session with a conditioning session — keep them roughly equal in volume.
Swap one conditioning or hypertrophy day for the axis this goal cares about.
Add one more short session this week, keeping every set 2–3 reps shy of failure.
Cut the heaviest set off each exercise for a week and prioritize recovery.
Revisit the target date — either extend it or close the goal and start a fresh one.
Log a workout to see your muscle load distribution.
Note: stimulus-weighted load. Compounds get a small boost; tiny shares are damped; small/sensitive muscles are capped.
If this is the goal, keep it 2–3 weeks. If not: add 2–4 sets/week for %1$s until rest share is above ~25%%.
If this is the goal, keep it 2–3 weeks. If not: add 2–4 sets/week to the least trained group until rest share is above ~25%%.
Good split. To avoid lag: keep 1–3 sets/week for %1$s and other low-share groups.
Good split. To avoid lag: keep 1–3 sets/week for low-share groups.
Solid base. If progress stalls: pick one group and add 2–3 sets/week.
If you want it cleaner: add 1–2 sets per session to %1$s (or whichever is lowest) until top-2 is ~70–75%%.
If you want it cleaner: add 1–2 sets per session to the lowest-share group until top-2 is ~70–75%%.
Volume is higher than stimulus for %1$s. Lots of tonnage, less focus. For size: add 1–2 back-off sets. For strength: keep it, but keep one heavy top set.
Stimulus is higher than volume for %1$s. More effort with less tonnage. For strength: add a heavier top set. For size: keep it and add 1 set.
Stimulus and volume match for the top group.
Assist groups look quiet — expected. The score dampens tiny shares, so compounds don\'t inflate arms or shoulders. If you want them to grow, add 2–6 isolation sets/week.
Low tail: remaining groups ~%1$d%%. %2$s ~%3$d%%. Consider a short maintenance block.
Low tail: remaining groups ~%1$d%%. Consider a short maintenance block.
A few quick answers help us tailor your training. You can change it anytime.
Small details help Grippo focus on what matters most for your training.
Naming your goal makes each session more intentional.
As you train, your goal becomes a steady anchor we can grow with.
